items.q: What is the difference between the activity of a source and the dose received by a person?
items.correct: Activity (Bq) is the rate of nuclear decays in the source; dose (Sv) measures the biological effect of absorbed radiation on tissue.
items.misconception: Activity and dose are the same — both measure how dangerous the radiation is.
items.random_wrong: Activity is for nuclear power stations and dose is for medical X-rays.
items.why: Students conflate 'how much radiation is emitted' with 'how much radiation a person absorbs', missing the role of distance, shielding and tissue sensitivity.

items.q: Which type of nuclear radiation is most ionising?
items.correct: Alpha radiation — large, doubly charged particles cause the most ionisation per unit distance.
items.misconception: Gamma is most ionising because it can travel furthest and pass through almost anything.
items.random_wrong: Beta radiation is most ionising because it consists of fast electrons.
items.why: Students conflate penetrating power with ionising power — gamma penetrates most because it ionises least, the opposite of intuition.

items.q: Why does light bend when it enters a denser medium like glass?
items.correct: Because it slows down — the change in speed at the boundary causes the wavefront to bend (towards the normal).
items.misconception: Because the medium is denser, it physically blocks part of the light and forces it to turn.
items.random_wrong: Because gravity in the glass pulls the light down.
items.why: Students attach refraction to 'density' as a material property without linking it to the change in wave speed and Snell's law.

items.q: An ideal gas at constant volume has its absolute temperature doubled. Its pressure:
items.why: Correct answer: Doubles. “Halves” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: pV = constant. “V/T = constant” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
